title-group.article-title: Ivonescimab combined with chemotherapy for the treatment of metastatic cervical cancer: A case report and literature review

contrib-group.aff: Department of Radiation Oncology, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, Qingdao, Shandong, China

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Yongchun Zhang zyc18661805058@163.com Department of Radiation Oncology, The Affiliated Hospital of Qingdao University, No. 16, Jiangsu Road, Shinan District, Qingdao, Shandong 266061, China.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: The prognosis for patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer (r/mCC) remains challenging, with a 5-y survival rate of approximately 17%. Although first-line treatment has evolved from traditional single-agent chemotherapy to combination regimens that incorporate targeted therapy and immunotherapy-showing improved initial response rates – therapeutic options after disease progression remain limited and often yield suboptimal outcomes. Ivonescimab, the first tetravalent bispecific monoclonal antibody capable of simultaneously targeting programmed death receptor-1 (PD-1) and vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A), demonstrates robust anti-tumor activity and a manageable safety profile across various solid tumors through synergistic blockade of both immune checkpoint pathways and tumor angiogenesis. This case report describes a patient diagnosed with cervical squamous cell carcinoma accompanied by hepatic metastases who received first-line therapy with platinum-based chemotherapy in combination with cadonilimab and bevacizumab. According to RECIST 1.1 criteria, a partial response (PR) was achieved after four treatment cycles, with a PFS of 11 months. Subsequent to disease progression, second-line treatment was initiated with ivonescimab combined with single-agent albumin-bound paclitaxel. After two treatment cycles, PR was achieved, and the patient continues to derive clinical benefits. Throughout the treatment course, the patient experienced only low-grade adverse events (CTCAE grade 1 or below) and maintained a satisfactory quality of life. To our knowledge, this is the first reported case demonstrating a significant response to ivonescimab in recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer following failure of first-line immunotherapy combined with anti-angiogenic therapy. This case preliminarily validates its anti-tumor activity and safety characteristics, and presents a novel therapeutic strategy for later-line management.

funding-group.funding-statement: The author(s) reported there is no funding associated with the work featured in this article.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Cervical cancer is among the most prevalent malignant tumors affecting women. While patients diagnosed at an early stage can achieve favorable therapeutic outcomes through surgical intervention, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy, the disease is associated with a relatively high rate of recurrence and metastasis, estimated at approximately 30%. The metastasis of cervical cancer primarily occurs through hematogenous and lymphatic pathways. Cervical squamous cell carcinoma predominantly disseminates via the lymphatic system, with common metastatic sites including the pelvic lymph nodes, vaginal region, and lateral pelvic walls.1 In contrast, patients with lymphatic metastasis exhibit a higher survival rate compared to those with hematogenous metastasis, which is typically associated with reduced treatment responsiveness and inferior overall survival.2 Moreover, the prognosis for patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer is extremely poor, with a 5-y survival rate of only about 17%, and the overall survival outcome is not satisfactory. Thus, the treatment of recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer continues to face significant challenges.3

In the management of recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, the therapeutic efficacy of conventional systemic chemotherapy remains suboptimal. Over the past decade, the combination of immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., PD-1/PD-L1 monoclonal antibodies) and anti-angiogenic agents (e.g., bevacizumab) has emerged as a pivotal treatment strategy for patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer.4 The treatment paradigm for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer is currently shifting from traditional single-agent chemotherapy to combinations of targeted therapy and immunotherapy. The integration of bevacizumab with PD-1/PD-L1 monoclonal antibodies has demonstrated improved survival outcomes in patients with recurrent and metastatic cervical cancer. The KEYNOTE-826 trial confirmed that this therapeutic regimen significantly prolongs both PFS and OS in this patient population.5 Moreover, in patients with PD-L1 negative status, the promising results from the Phase III COMPASSION-16 clinical trial have introduced a novel therapeutic option for the first-line treatment of recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer.6 However, subsequent treatment options following disease progression after first-line combination therapy remain limited. The effectiveness of conventional chemotherapy or single-agent immunotherapy is suboptimal, highlighting an urgent need for the exploration of novel therapeutic strategies. PD-1/VEGF bispecific monoclonal antibodies represent a major advancement in oncology in recent years. By concurrently targeting both the PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint and the VEGF/VEGFR signaling pathway, these agents have the potential to more effectively modulate the tumor immune-suppressive microenvironment and suppress angiogenesis, thereby addressing the limitations of drug resistance associated with monotherapy or traditional combination approaches.7,8

This case report describes a patient diagnosed with recurrent and recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer who achieved a clinically meaningful response following a switch to the PD-1/VEGF bispecific monoclonal antibody ivonescimab after disease progression on first-line chemotherapy combined with PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific monoclonal antibody cadonilimab and anti-angiogenic therapy. Dynamic imaging assessments and safety analyses were conducted to evaluate the potential therapeutic value of ivonescimab in recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, providing preliminary evidence to support its consideration in later-line treatment strategies.

sec.title: Case report
sec.p:
On December 13, 2023, a 75-y-old female presented to our hospital with a chief complaint of irregular vaginal bleeding persisting for over six months. She reported no family history of tumors and had previously sought evaluation at an external hospital without receiving specific treatment. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) revealed cervical thickening with a hypermetabolic fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) focus measuring 12 × 13 mm, consistent with a malignant lesion (Figure 1A). Multiple mildly hypermetabolic lymph nodes in the pelvic cavity suggested possible metastatic involvement. Several hypermetabolic foci were also detected in the liver, indicating potential hepatic metastasis (Figure 1B). Additionally, metabolically active small lymph nodes were identified in the abdominal cavity and retroperitoneum, with metastasis not excluded. According to the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) staging system, the patient was diagnosed with stage IVB cervical squamous cell carcinoma with concomitant liver and lymph node metastases. A cervical biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of cervical squamous cell carcinoma. Immunohistochemical staining demonstrated a programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) tumor proportion score (TPS) of <1% (Figures 1C-D). The patient’s performance status was Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) Grade 1. Figure 1.
Representative baseline diagnostic evaluation was conducted prior to the initiation of treatment. (A) PET-CT demonstrated cervical thickening accompanied by a hypermetabolic FDG lesion measuring 12 × 13 mm, consistent with malignancy. Multiple mildly hypermetabolic pelvic lymph nodes were identified, indicative of possible metastatic involvement. (B) Multiple hypermetabolic foci were detected in the liver, suggesting potential metastatic lesions. (C) Histopathological examination following hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining confirmed poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (original magnification, ×100). (D) Immunohistochemical analysis revealed PD-L1 expression with a tumor proportion score (TPS) of less than 1%.

Based on the 2023 clinical practice guidelines from the Chinese Society of Clinical Oncology (CSCO) and the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN), the patient was diagnosed with advanced cervical squamous cell carcinoma. Platinum-based chemotherapy combined with bevacizumab and pembrolizumab is recognized as the preferred first-line therapeutic approach. The 2023 CSCO guidelines include both pembrolizumab and cadonilimab as recommended agents for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer. Following a comprehensive evaluation of the patient’s condition and the guideline recommendations, the TP regimen (paclitaxel plus cisplatin) combined with cadonilimab and bevacizumab was selected as first-line treatment.

After two cycles of treatment, the primary lesion showed reduction in size compared to baseline, and the number of intrahepatic metastases decreased. Upon completion of four cycles, dynamic contrast-enhanced CT of the upper abdomen revealed complete remission of the intrahepatic metastatic lesions. Pelvic magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed further shrinkage of the primary tumor to approximately 9 × 9 mm. According to the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors (RECIST) version 1.1, the overall therapeutic response was assessed as partial response (PR) (Figure 2 A2, B2). By March 2024, a total of five treatment cycles were completed, and the patient subsequently received maintenance therapy with cadonilimab and bevacizumab. Figure 2.
(A1, B1) Represent baseline assessments prior to treatment initiation. Following four cycles of therapy: (A2) dynamic contrast-enhanced CT of the upper abdomen demonstrated complete remission of intrahepatic metastatic lesions. (B2) Pelvic MRI evidenced further shrinkage of the primary tumor.

After clinical assessment, radiotherapy targeting the retroperitoneal and pelvic regions was initiated on April 11, 2024. The gross tumor volume of the nodal sites (GTV-n) received a total dose of 59 Gy, while the clinical target volume (CTV) received 46 Gy, delivered in 28 fractions. This was combined with four sessions of brachytherapy. Bevacizumab was withheld during radiotherapy, whereas cadonilimab was maintained throughout. Radiotherapy was completed on June 19, 2024, with no significant adverse events such as diarrhea observed during treatment. Post-radiotherapy, the primary lesion remained stable, and no new hepatic lesions were identified. The patient resumed maintenance therapy with immunotherapy combined with anti-angiogenic therapy and was followed up regularly.

On November 21, 2024, subsequent MRI revealed multiple slightly hypodense lesions in the liver, highly suggestive of metastases, indicating the emergence of new metastatic foci. According to RECIST 1.1, the disease was evaluated as progressive disease (PD) (Figure 3 A1). A transition to second-line therapy was recommended. Considering the favorable initial response to the first-line immunotherapy combined with anti-angiogenic therapy and in alignment with the 2024 CSCO and NCCN guidelines, albumin-bound paclitaxel monotherapy was recommended as the subsequent treatment. Following a comprehensive discussion among the medical team, the patient, and her family, a combination regimen of albumin-bound paclitaxel and ivonescimab was initiated on December 21, 2024. Due to poor tolerance to chemotherapy, the patient was switched to ivonescimab monotherapy after completing one treatment cycle. After three cycles, follow-up imaging demonstrated a reduction in the size of the intrahepatic lesions, and the therapeutic response was assessed as PR (Figure 3 A2). Following six cycles of therapy, further tumor shrinkage was observed, with the response sustained as PR (Figure 3 A3). During this period, the patient maintained a satisfactory quality of life, and no significant adverse events were observed. The overall treatment timeline is summarized in Figure 4. Currently, the patient continues maintenance therapy with ivonescimab. Figure 3.
(A1) An upper abdominal MRI conducted on November 21, 2024, revealed newly developed hepatic metastatic lesions. (A2) Following completion of the third cycle of second-line therapy. (A3) After completion of the sixth cycle of second-line therapy. (B1–B3) The primary cervical lesion demonstrated favorable local control. Figure 4.
The treatment timeline for the patient.

Throughout the systemic treatment and radiotherapy periods, the patient underwent continuous adverse event monitoring. All observed adverse events were regularly assessed according to the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (CTCAE) version 5.0, with all recorded events being Grade 1 or below in severity. No adverse reactions of Grade 2 or higher occurred.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: We present a case report of a patient who experienced disease progression following first-line chemotherapy combined with cadonilimab and bevacizumab, but subsequently achieved a favorable therapeutic response with treatment using a PD-1/VEGF bispecific monoclonal antibody (ivonescimab). Currently, platinum-based chemotherapy remains the standard first-line treatment for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, despite its limited clinical efficacy. Results from the GOG240 Phase III trial indicated that the addition of the anti-angiogenic agent bevacizumab into platinum-based chemotherapy could significantly prolong the median OS from 13.3 months to 16.8 months.9 In the KEYNOTE-826 trial, the combination of the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab with standard chemotherapy was shown to extend the median OS to 26.4 months in patients with programmed death ligand 1 (PD-L1)-positive tumors.5 The above research evidence has established bevacizumab and pembrolizumab as supplementary options to the standard first-line treatment for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer.

However, the efficacy of pembrolizumab in PD-L1 negative patients is quite limited. Cadonilimab is a PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific antibody independently developed in China and is the first of its kind to be approved globally. It can simultaneously target the two immune checkpoints, PD-1 and CTLA-4, and enhance the anti-tumor immune response by dual blocking of T-cell inhibitory signals. In the Phase III COMPASSION-16 study, cadonilimab demonstrated outstanding efficacy as a first-line treatment for untreated recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer patients, with a median progression-free survival (mPFS) of 12.7 months in the cadonilimab group.6 It is noteworthy that it also demonstrated significant survival benefits in PD-L1 negative patients.5 In the COMPASSION-16 subgroup analysis, the mPFS in the cadonilimab group was also 12 months for patients with a PD-L1 CPS < 1.10 Due to the limited efficacy of traditional PD-1/PD-L1 monotherapy in PD-L1-negative tumors, cadonilimab demonstrated a significantly higher objective response rate (ORR) in PD-L1-negative patients compared to conventional single-target PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer. However, this improvement may be associated with a notable increase in immune-related adverse events (irAEs).11 After a comprehensive assessment of all relevant clinical factors-including the patient’s metastatic site (e.g., lung/liver metastasis), PD-L1 expression status, performance status (ECOG PS), prior treatment history, and baseline organ function (e.g., renal/hepatic function)-we formulated an individualized treatment strategy : the combination of the TP regimen with cadonilimab and bevacizumab. Following four cycles of therapy, reevaluation confirmed complete remission (CR) of liver metastases, and PFS reached 11 months – approaching, although not surpassing, the median PFS reported in the COMPASSION-16 study. Based on these observations, we propose that long-term maintenance therapy with cadonilimab in combination with bevacizumab may offer enhanced clinical benefits. This case further serves as real-world evidence supporting the findings of existing clinical research.

Concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CCRT) is the standard treatment for locally advanced cervical cancer. However, for patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, radical pelvic radiotherapy combined with systemic therapy can improve survival outcomes, especially for those with a good performance status.12 Moreover, research has found that local radiotherapy combined with chemotherapy ± bevacizumab can improve the efficacy and survival rate of patients with advanced and recurrent metastatic cervical cancer.13 Even for patients with M1 stage disease and extensive metastases (≥2 metastatic foci), radiotherapy can still improve their survival (P < .001).14,15 Additionally, research has shown that changes in the tumor immune microenvironment after radiotherapy can induce the expression of PD-L1.16 Thus, it can produce a synergistic effect with immune checkpoint inhibitors.17 From this case, we also observed the benefits of local radiotherapy. The liver metastases that did not receive radiotherapy developed metastases during maintenance treatment, while the lesions treated with radiotherapy achieved good local control. It is evident that local radiotherapy in recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer has the dual role of local control and potential survival benefit. However, the specific timing and extent of radiotherapy still require further exploration.

In addition, the selection of second-line treatment for patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer remains a critical clinical challenge, particularly following the development of resistance to first-line therapy. Currently, there is a scarcity of head – to – head clinical trials that directly compare diverse second – line regimens. Consequently, therapeutic decisions are typically informed by the patient’s prior treatment history, biomarker status, and overall organ function reserve. As a pioneering tetravalent bispecific monoclonal antibody, ivonescimab currently lacks definitive clinical evidence supporting its use in advanced cervical cancer. Nevertheless, its potential as part of combination strategies involving targeted therapy and immunotherapy merits further investigation.

Studies have shown that the VEGF/VEGFR signaling pathway affects the activity of immune cells such as regulatory T cells (Tregs), tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), thereby inducing the aggregation of regulatory T cells and influencing the response of T cells.18,19 In addition, VEGF can induce the expression of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α), which directly upregulates immune checkpoint molecules, such as PD-1 and PD-L1, thereby promoting immune evasion. Furthermore, VEGF disrupts the migration and infiltration of immune cells into the tumor microenvironment by forming of a “vascular immune barrier,” ultimately suppressing the anti-tumor immune response.20 The blockade of the VEGF/VEGFR pathway has been demonstrated to exert a synergistic effect with immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), such as PD-1 and PD-L1 inhibitors. Anti-angiogenic agents can normalize tumor vasculature, thereby reducing vascular leakage and hypoxia. This not only facilitates the remodeling of the tumor immune microenvironment, but also suppresses HIF-1α expression through the alleviation of hypoxic conditions. Consequently, the production of immune checkpoint molecules is diminished, allowing ICIs to more effectively activate T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity.21–23 Current research has demonstrated that blocking the VEGF/VEGFR signaling pathway can enhance the anti-tumor efficacy of anti-PD-1/PD-L1 antibodies in various preclinical cancer models.19

Bispecific antibodies (BsAbs) are a class of rationally engineered antibody constructs designed to simultaneously recognizing and binding to two distinct antigens or epitopes-an inherent structural feature that differentiates them from traditional monospecific antibodies. Owing to their distinctive structural design and functional properties,BsAbs exhibit notable therapeutic advantages, with particular transformative potential in oncology, where their dual-targeting capacity addresses unmet needs in tumor treatment.24 Ivonescimab is a humanized, tetravalent bispecific monoclonal antibody that simultaneously targets PD-1 and VEGF-A. It restores the anti-tumor activity of T cells while inhibiting tumor angiogenesis, achieving a synergistic effect of immune microenvironment remodeling and anti-angiogenesis. It exerts anti-tumor effects through dual-pathway blockade.8,25 Furthermore, the dual-antibody design may circumvent the pharmacokinetic differences of traditional combination therapy, thereby enhancing the efficiency of drug delivery.7 Notably, VEGF pathway activation contributes to immunosuppression within the tumor microenvironment (TME), representing a well-established mechanism underlying resistance to PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors. By concurrently blocking VEGF, ivonescimab may alleviate TME immunosuppression and thereby restore antitumor immune responsiveness.25 Moreover, emerging evidence suggests that specific molecular alterations in the tumor microenvironment – including ECM3+ expression and aberrant activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway – are mechanistically linked to primary or acquired resistance to PD-1/PD-L1 blockade. By concurrently inhibiting PD-1 and VEGF-A, ivonescimab may overcome such resistance through multimodal remodeling of the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, including normalization of tumor vasculature, enhancement of T-cell infiltration, and attenuation of stromal barrier functions.26 Emerging clinical evidence suggests that combining PD-1/VEGF dual blockade with stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) may improve disease control in patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) who have developed resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs).27 In the HARMONi-A Phase III study, ivonescimab combined with chemotherapy (pemetrexed + carboplatin) significantly prolonged PFS of patients (7.1 months vs. 4.8 months, P < .001), and increased ORR by 15.6%.28 In the HARMONI-2 study, among previously untreated patients with advanced PD-L1 positive non-small cell lung cancer, ivonescimab significantly improved PFS compared with pembrolizumab (11.1 months vs. 5.8 months, p < .0001).29 Currently, ivonescimab has been approved in China for the treatment of patients with locally advanced or metastatic non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer(NSCLC) with EGFR mutations who have progressed after tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) therapy. Additionally, it is being investigated for its efficacy in other solid tumors. In the treatment platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, PR was 26.3%. In multiple solid tumors such as mismatch repair proficient (pMMR) colorectal cancer and endometrial cancer, it has shown controllable safety and promising efficacy signals.25 Although there is currently a lack of clinical data on the application of ivonescimab in cervical cancer, multiple clinical studies have shown that the combination of anti-angiogenic drugs (such as bevacizumab) and immune checkpoint inhibitors (such as pembrolizumab) can significantly prolong PFS and OS.30–32 In our clinical case, the patient was administered ivonescimab following disease progression after first-line therapy, resulting in a notable therapeutic response. No serious adverse events were observed, thereby offering a promising and well-tolerated option for subsequent lines of treatment.

Although this case provides preliminary evidence supporting the potential role of ivonescimab as a later-line treatment option for recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, the findings should be interpreted with caution due to the inherent limitations of single-case reporting. To date, there has been a lack of dedicated clinical trials evaluating the efficacy and safety of ivonescimab specifically in cervical cancer. Key clinical questions, such as the optimal timing of administration, criteria for patient selection, and the long-term safety profile, require further investigation. Therefore, well-designed, large-scale clinical trials are warranted to rigorously assess the true therapeutic potential and safety of ivonescimab in patients with recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer.

bio.p: Yongchun Zhang, Graduated from the Department of Radiation Oncology of Bethune Medical University, I have been engaged in medical, teaching and research work in radiation oncology for 22 years. I have been committed to the radiotherapy and comprehensive treatment of malignant tumors. I am proficient in the radiotherapy and comprehensive treatment of gynecological tumors (such as cervical cancer and endometrial cancer), thoracic tumors and pelvic tumors. In the radiotherapy of cervical cancer, breast cancer and rectal cancer, I have developed many new technologies that are leading in the province and advanced in the country. I focus on the clinical and scientific research of gynecological tumors such as cervical cancer. As the first author or corresponding author, I have published more than 20 papers in SCI journals and domestic Chinese Class I journals. I have 3 invention patents. I am currently in charge of 5 ongoing research projects. I am a member of the Gynecological Tumor Group of the Chinese Society of Radiation Oncology, a member of the Brachytherapy Group of the Chinese Society of Radiation Oncology, and the vice chairman of the Radiation Oncology Professional Committee of the Shandong Anti-Cancer Association. I am also a member of the Radiation Oncology Professional Committee of the Chinese Medical Doctor Association, the deputy group leader of the Radioimmunology Group of the Shandong Anti-Cancer Association, and the vice chairman of the Radiation Physics and Technology Branch of the Qingdao Medical Association.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: Ethical approval was not applicable as this research did not involve human or animal participants.
